question: How is GitHub Copilot best described?

options.text: An AI-powered coding assistant ("AI pair programmer") that suggests and generates code and answers questions in your editor
A code formatter that enforces a fixed style guide
A CI/CD service that builds and deploys your repositories
A package manager for installing open-source dependencies

explanation: GitHub Copilot is an AI-powered coding assistant—an "AI pair programmer"—that helps you generate, understand, refactor, and debug code using inline suggestions and a chat experience.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Introduction to GitHub Copilot / AI pair programmer

question: GitHub Copilot was developed in collaboration with which company and is originally powered by which system?

options.text: OpenAI, powered by the OpenAI Codex system
Google, powered by the Gemini system
Anthropic, powered by the Claude system
Meta, powered by the Llama system

explanation: Microsoft developed GitHub Copilot in collaboration with OpenAI, and it is powered by the OpenAI Codex system, which was trained on a large concentration of public source code.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Introduction to GitHub Copilot / AI pair programmer

question: In which of the following environments is GitHub Copilot available as an extension? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: Visual Studio Code
Visual Studio
Vim/Neovim
The JetBrains suite of IDEs
Microsoft Word

explanation: GitHub Copilot is available as an extension for VS Code, Visual Studio, Vim/Neovim, and the JetBrains suite of IDEs.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Introduction to GitHub Copilot / AI pair programmer

question: What does the Copilot chat experience inside a supported editor let you do?

options.text: Ask questions about your code, get explanations of logic or errors, and generate tests or documentation using your project context
Only autocomplete a single line of code at a time with no conversation
Replace your editor's source control with a built-in Git client
Deploy your application to a cloud provider

explanation: Copilot chat is an interactive experience in supported editors. It understands your code context so you can ask questions, get explanations of logic or errors, and generate tests, documentation, or implementation ideas.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Introduction to GitHub Copilot / AI pair programmer

question: When you open a pull request, how can GitHub Copilot help reviewers?

options.text: It can automatically generate a summary description of the changes
It automatically merges the pull request once checks pass
It deletes the source branch after review
It assigns reviewers based on repository billing

explanation: Copilot can automatically generate a pull request summary of the changes, helping reviewers understand intent and reducing the effort of writing PR descriptions.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Introduction to GitHub Copilot / AI pair programmer

question: What is the purpose of the GitHub Copilot command-line interface (CLI)?

options.text: To ask for command suggestions, generate shell scripts, and understand command output or errors in the terminal
To replace Git as the version control system
To host static websites from the terminal
To manage organization billing from the command line

explanation: Copilot for the CLI helps in the terminal: you can ask for command suggestions and code snippets, generate shell scripts, and understand output or errors.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Introduction to GitHub Copilot / AI pair programmer

question: How do inline suggestions appear, and how do you accept one in your editor?

options.text: They appear as grayed-out text ahead of the cursor, and you accept by pressing the Tab key
They appear in a popup dialog, and you accept by pressing Enter twice
They appear highlighted in red, and you accept by pressing Esc
They appear only in the chat panel, and you accept by clicking Submit

explanation: Inline suggestions show as grayed-out text ahead of your cursor. Press Tab (or the right-arrow key) to accept; keep typing or press Esc to reject.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Introduction to GitHub Copilot / Interact with Copilot

question: How do you reject an inline suggestion that Copilot offers?

options.text: Keep typing or press the Esc key
Press the Tab key
Press the right-arrow key
Save the file

explanation: To reject an inline suggestion, simply keep typing or press Esc. Tab and the right-arrow key accept the suggestion.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Introduction to GitHub Copilot / Interact with Copilot

question: What is inline chat in GitHub Copilot used for?

options.text: Context-specific conversations directly within the editor to request code changes or explanations without switching context
Chatting with other developers in your organization in real time
Posting questions to the public GitHub Community forum
Sending email summaries of your commits

explanation: Inline chat (opened with Ctrl+I / Cmd+I) enables context-specific conversations within the editor so you can request modifications or explanations for a specific code location without switching contexts.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Introduction to GitHub Copilot / Interact with Copilot

question: In Visual Studio Code, where can you find Copilot actions such as "Explain This" or "Generate Unit Tests"?

options.text: The command palette (Ctrl+Shift+P / Cmd+Shift+P), by typing "Copilot"
The repository Settings page on GitHub.com
The Git blame view
The organization billing dashboard

explanation: Open the command palette with Ctrl+Shift+P (Cmd+Shift+P on Mac), type "Copilot," and choose actions like "Explain This" or "Generate Unit Tests."
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Introduction to GitHub Copilot / Interact with Copilot

question: Which GitHub Copilot plan lets an individual developer use Copilot at no cost with limited monthly code completions and chat requests?

options.text: GitHub Copilot Free
GitHub Copilot Business
GitHub Copilot Enterprise
GitHub Copilot Pro+

explanation: GitHub Copilot Free lets individual developers use Copilot at no cost, including limited monthly completions and chat requests plus access to supported AI models.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Introduction to GitHub Copilot / Subscription plans

question: An organization wants centralized policy management, public-code filtering, and IP indemnity for its developers' Copilot usage. Which plan provides these?

options.text: GitHub Copilot Business
GitHub Copilot Free
GitHub Copilot Pro
GitHub Copilot Pro+

explanation: GitHub Copilot Business adds centralized management and policy controls, security vulnerability filtering, code referencing / public code filtering, and IP indemnity—features aimed at organizations.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Introduction to GitHub Copilot / Subscription plans

question: Which capability is unique to GitHub Copilot Enterprise (beyond what Business offers)?

options.text: Personalized code suggestions based on the organization's internal/private code by indexing its codebase
Inline code completions in the editor
Access to Copilot chat in the IDE
The ability to reject suggestions with the Esc key

explanation: Copilot Enterprise includes everything in Business plus personalization—it can index an organization's codebase to give tailored suggestions, AI-powered search/documentation, and deep GitHub integration.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Introduction to GitHub Copilot / Subscription plans

question: What distinguishes GitHub Copilot Pro+ from GitHub Copilot Pro?

options.text: Pro+ adds additional premium model request capacity and priority infrastructure access on top of all Pro features
Pro+ removes the ability to use Copilot chat
Pro+ is free while Pro is paid
Pro+ only works in the CLI, not in editors

explanation: Copilot Pro+ includes all Pro features plus additional premium request capacity and priority access to high-demand infrastructure, for individuals with higher usage needs.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Introduction to GitHub Copilot / Subscription plans

question: What are Microsoft and GitHub's six principles of Responsible AI?

options.text: Fairness; Reliability and safety; Privacy and security; Inclusiveness; Transparency; Accountability
Speed; Scalability; Security; Simplicity; Savings; Support
Accuracy; Automation; Availability; Auditing; Authentication; Authorization
Fairness; Profitability; Performance; Privacy; Portability; Predictability

explanation: The six principles are Fairness, Reliability and safety, Privacy and security, Inclusiveness, Transparency, and Accountability.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Responsible AI with GitHub Copilot / Six principles

question: Which Responsible AI principle states that AI systems should treat all people fairly and avoid differential impacts on similarly situated groups?

options.text: Fairness
Transparency
Accountability
Reliability and safety

explanation: Fairness means AI systems should treat everyone fairly, giving consistent recommendations to people in similar situations and using techniques to detect and mitigate bias.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Responsible AI with GitHub Copilot / Six principles

question: The Responsible AI principle that AI systems should be understandable maps to which principle?

options.text: Transparency
Inclusiveness
Privacy and security
Fairness

explanation: Transparency means AI systems should be understandable—people should be able to comprehend how decisions are made.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Responsible AI with GitHub Copilot / Six principles

question: Under the Responsible AI principles, who should be accountable for AI systems?

options.text: People should be accountable for AI systems
The AI model itself is solely accountable
Only the end users are accountable
No accountability is required for AI systems

explanation: Accountability means people—not the AI—are responsible for how AI systems operate and the outcomes they produce.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Responsible AI with GitHub Copilot / Six principles

question: Which Responsible AI principle focuses on AI systems performing consistently as intended and responding safely to unexpected conditions?

options.text: Reliability and safety
Inclusiveness
Transparency
Accountability

explanation: Reliability and safety means AI systems must operate reliably, safely, and consistently—functioning as designed, handling unexpected conditions safely, and resisting harmful manipulation.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Responsible AI with GitHub Copilot / Six principles

question: Which Responsible AI principle is concerned with AI systems empowering everyone and engaging people, regardless of ability or background?

options.text: Inclusiveness
Privacy and security
Reliability and safety
Accountability

explanation: Inclusiveness means AI systems should empower and engage everyone, avoiding the exclusion of any group of people.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Responsible AI with GitHub Copilot / Six principles

question: Which of the 4 Ss principles of GitHub Copilot prompt engineering is described as 'utilizing descriptive filenames and keeping related files open to provide rich context'?

options.text: Surround
Single
Specific
Short

explanation: The 'Surround' principle directs developers to use descriptive filenames and keep related files open so that GitHub Copilot receives rich context, leading to more tailored code suggestions.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Introduction to Prompt Engineering with GitHub Copilot / Prompt engineering foundations and best practices

question: A developer provides GitHub Copilot with a single example of a temperature conversion function and then asks it to generate another similar function following the same pattern. Which learning approach is being used?

options.text: One-shot learning
Zero-shot learning
Few-shot learning
LoRA fine-tuning

explanation: One-shot learning involves providing a single example to help Copilot generate code that follows the developer's specific patterns and conventions, making it effective for creating consistent implementations.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Introduction to Prompt Engineering with GitHub Copilot / Prompt engineering foundations and best practices

question: What is the primary purpose of 'role prompting' when working with GitHub Copilot?

options.text: To instruct Copilot to act as a specific type of expert, improving the quality and relevance of generated code for specialized domains
To assign different permission levels to Copilot based on the developer's job title
To configure Copilot to only respond to prompts from users with admin roles
To set Copilot into a read-only code review mode

explanation: Role prompting involves instructing Copilot to assume the perspective of a specific expert (such as a security expert or testing specialist), which generates more targeted, domain-appropriate code on the first try.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Introduction to Prompt Engineering with GitHub Copilot / Prompt engineering foundations and best practices

question: During GitHub Copilot's inbound prompt processing, which pre-processing technique is used to consider both the preceding and following code context in order to expand the model's understanding?

options.text: Fill-in-the-Middle (FIM)
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting
Beam Search decoding

explanation: The Fill-in-the-Middle (FIM) technique pre-processes both the code before and after the cursor position, effectively expanding the model's understanding so it can generate more accurate and relevant suggestions.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Introduction to Prompt Engineering with GitHub Copilot / GitHub Copilot user prompt process flow

question: In GitHub Copilot's outbound post-processing validation, which types of security vulnerabilities are specifically checked in generated code responses?

options.text: Cross-site scripting (XSS) and SQL injection
Buffer overflows and memory leaks
Race conditions and deadlocks
Circular dependencies and unused imports

explanation: During outbound post-processing, Copilot checks responses for common bugs or vulnerabilities specifically including cross-site scripting (XSS) and SQL injection to ensure generated code is robust and secure.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Introduction to Prompt Engineering with GitHub Copilot / GitHub Copilot user prompt process flow

question: How long does GitHub typically retain prompts, suggestions, and supporting context for Copilot Chat used outside the code editor?

options.text: 28 days
7 days
14 days
90 days

explanation: For Copilot Chat used outside the code editor (including CLI, Mobile, and GitHub.com), GitHub typically retains prompts, suggestions, and supporting context for 28 days.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Introduction to Prompt Engineering with GitHub Copilot / GitHub Copilot data

question: What option is available to GitHub Copilot Individual subscribers regarding how their prompts are used?

options.text: They can opt out of sharing their prompts with GitHub, which would otherwise be used to fine-tune the foundational model
They can configure a custom data retention period for their prompts
They can request that all prompts be processed locally on their machine rather than on GitHub's servers
They can permanently delete all previously submitted prompts from GitHub's servers at any time

explanation: GitHub Copilot Individual subscribers have the option to opt out of sharing their prompts with GitHub; by default, those prompts would be used to fine-tune GitHub's foundational model.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Introduction to Prompt Engineering with GitHub Copilot / GitHub Copilot data

question: What is the key advantage of LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) fine-tuning compared to traditional full fine-tuning of a large language model?

options.text: It adds smaller trainable parts to each layer of the pretrained model rather than retraining the entire model, saving time and resources
It retrains all neural network layers from scratch, producing more accurate results than partial retraining
It converts the model into a compressed format that runs entirely on the developer's local machine
It requires significantly larger task-specific datasets than standard fine-tuning methods

explanation: LoRA adds smaller trainable parts to each layer of the pretrained model while leaving the original model unchanged, which saves time and compute resources compared to full fine-tuning, and outperforms other adaptation methods like adapters and prefix-tuning.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Introduction to Prompt Engineering with GitHub Copilot / GitHub Copilot Large Language Models (LLMs)

question: When configuring a Copilot Space, what is the distinction between 'Instructions' and 'Attachments'?

options.text: Instructions are free text describing what Copilot should focus on; attachments are content (files, issues, PRs, uploads, text) used to provide more relevant answers
Instructions link GitHub issues and pull requests; attachments set visibility and permission levels
Instructions define the AI model to use in the Space; attachments upload code from local machine only
Instructions set the Space owner; attachments store the conversation history

explanation: Instructions are free text that tell Copilot what to focus on, its areas of expertise, and what to avoid. Attachments are the actual content (files, folders, issues, PRs, uploads, or free text) that give Copilot relevant information to ground its responses.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Introduction to Copilot Spaces / Creating your first space

question: What is the advantage of creating an organization-owned Copilot Space rather than a personal one?

options.text: Organization-owned Spaces can be shared using GitHub's built-in permission model
Organization-owned Spaces have a larger context window than personal Spaces
Only organization-owned Spaces can attach files and folders from repositories
Organization-owned Spaces automatically synchronize with every repository in the organization

explanation: When a Space is owned by an organization, it can be shared across the organization using GitHub's built-in permission model, making it accessible to relevant team members.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Introduction to Copilot Spaces / Creating your first space

question: When a file from a GitHub repository is attached to a Copilot Space, which version of the code does the Space always reference?

options.text: The latest version on the main (default) branch of the repository
The version of the file at the time it was attached to the Space
The version from the most recently merged pull request
Whichever branch was checked out when the file was added

explanation: Copilot Spaces always refer to the latest version of the code on the main branch of the repository, so attached files stay fresh as the codebase evolves without needing to re-attach them.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Introduction to Copilot Spaces / Creating your first space

question: How does a Copilot Space handle access when it links to private repositories, issues, or pull requests?

options.text: Only users who already have the appropriate repository permissions see that private material reflected in answers
The Space grants temporary read access to all members so they can see linked private content
Private repositories cannot be attached to any Copilot Space
Linked private content is anonymized so all users see the same redacted responses

explanation: A Space does not grant new access; it only surfaces content that viewers are already entitled to see based on their existing GitHub repository permissions, allowing safe sharing across an organization.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Introduction to Copilot Spaces / Sharing, Discoverability, and Governance

question: What is the recommended best practice for handling sensitive information when working with Copilot Spaces?

options.text: Avoid pasting sensitive data into free-text notes; prefer linking to version-controlled files where normal review and permissions apply
Encrypt all sensitive content before pasting it into the Space's instructions field
Store sensitive data only in attachments, never in the instructions field
Use password-protected file uploads for any content classified as sensitive

explanation: The module explicitly advises against pasting sensitive data into free-text notes and instead recommends linking to version-controlled files, where standard GitHub review and permissions processes apply.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Introduction to Copilot Spaces / Sharing, Discoverability, and Governance

question: According to Copilot Spaces best practices, what should you do when a Space grows beyond a single job or task domain?

options.text: Split it into smaller, focused Spaces to restore precision and predictability
Expand the Space's instructions to handle all additional jobs within a single Space
Archive the Space and create a brand-new one from scratch
Upgrade the subscription tier to increase the context limit for that Space

explanation: When a Space grows beyond a single job, the guidance is to split it into smaller Spaces so that discoverability stays high and answer quality remains consistent, preventing context limit issues and degraded responses.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Introduction to Copilot Spaces / Do's and Don'ts of Working in a Space

question: Which action is explicitly unsupported when chatting inside a Copilot Space?

options.text: @-mentioning people or invoking Copilot extensions
Asking questions about attached pull requests and issues
Uploading text files directly from your local machine
Adding free-text notes as Space context

explanation: The module explicitly states that @-mentioning people will not notify anyone, and Copilot extensions cannot be invoked from within a Space's chat. These actions are unsupported in that environment.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Introduction to Copilot Spaces / Do's and Don'ts of Working in a Space

question: In GitHub Copilot, what are AI-generated inline code suggestions called, and how does a developer accept them?

options.text: Ghost text; press Tab to accept
Phantoms; press Enter to accept
Drafts; press F2 to accept
Previews; press Spacebar to accept

explanation: GitHub Copilot refers to its inline AI-generated suggestions as 'ghost text.' Developers can accept a ghost text suggestion by pressing the Tab key.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Using Advanced GitHub Copilot Features / Advanced GitHub Copilot features

question: What keyboard shortcut opens GitHub Copilot's inline chat in Visual Studio Code on Windows, allowing developers to interact with Copilot without switching context to a different pane?

options.text: Ctrl+i
Ctrl+Shift+P
Ctrl+Alt+C
Ctrl+Shift+I

explanation: The inline chat in Visual Studio Code is accessed using Ctrl+i on Windows (or Command+i on Mac), enabling interaction with Copilot closer to the code without switching to a separate pane.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Using Advanced GitHub Copilot Features / Advanced GitHub Copilot features

question: What is the purpose of the @terminal agent in GitHub Copilot within Visual Studio Code?

options.text: It allows Copilot to provide suggestions and help based on the terminal's context and output
It opens an integrated terminal panel directly inside the Copilot chat pane
It automatically generates and runs deployment shell scripts from the chat interface
It monitors all terminal sessions and applies automatic error corrections without prompting

explanation: The @terminal agent lets developers chat with GitHub Copilot to interact with the terminal, allowing Copilot to provide suggestions based on terminal output—for example, asking 'How do I fix the error message I'm seeing?'
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Using Advanced GitHub Copilot Features / Advanced GitHub Copilot features

question: A developer selects a block of code containing a bug and opens the GitHub Copilot inline chat. Which slash command should they use to ask Copilot to correct the defect?

options.text: /fix
/optimize
/explain
/doc

explanation: The /fix slash command is used to correct bugs in selected code. The module demonstrates using /fix in the inline chat to repair a Python file.read bug, with Copilot providing the corrected method call.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Using Advanced GitHub Copilot Features / Applied GitHub Copilot techniques

question: Which GitHub Copilot Chat slash command is specifically designed to add comments or documentation to specified or selected code?

options.text: /doc
/explain
/generate
/help

explanation: The /doc slash command adds comments to the specified or selected code, whereas /explain is used to get explanations about what code does—these are two distinct commands with different purposes.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Using Advanced GitHub Copilot Features / Applied GitHub Copilot techniques

question: By default, what does GitHub Copilot use as additional context when generating code suggestions inside the editor?

options.text: The open files in your text editor
The project's README and package manifest files
The last 10 commits in the active repository
Only the single file currently being edited

explanation: By default, GitHub Copilot uses all open files in the text editor as additional context—not just the active file—to generate more relevant and accurate suggestions.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Using Advanced GitHub Copilot Features / Applied GitHub Copilot techniques

question: How many code autocompletes and chat messages does the GitHub Copilot Free tier provide per month?

options.text: 2,000 code autocompletes and 50 chat messages
1,000 code autocompletes and 100 chat messages
500 code autocompletes and 25 chat messages
Unlimited autocompletes and 50 chat messages

explanation: The GitHub Copilot Free tier offers exactly 2,000 code autocompletes and 50 chat messages per month. These usage limits distinguish Free from paid plans like Pro, Business, and Enterprise.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · GitHub Copilot Across Environments / Code completion with GitHub Copilot

question: On macOS, which keyboard shortcut cycles to the NEXT suggestion in GitHub Copilot's multiple suggestions pane?

options.text: Option (⌥) or Alt+]
Option (⌥) or Alt+[
Cmd+]
Ctrl+Enter

explanation: On macOS, Option (⌥) or Alt+] moves to the next suggestion, while Option (⌥) or Alt+[ moves to the previous suggestion. On Windows/Linux the same Alt+] / Alt+[ shortcuts apply.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · GitHub Copilot Across Environments / Code completion with GitHub Copilot

question: How many Premium Request Units (PRUs) does a premium AI model (such as o1-preview) consume per request in GitHub Copilot Chat, compared to the standard GPT-4o model?

options.text: 2 PRUs, double the 1 PRU consumed by the standard model
1 PRU, the same as the standard model
5 PRUs, five times the standard model rate
Premium models do not consume PRUs; only standard models do

explanation: Standard models like GPT-4o consume 1 PRU per request, while premium models such as o1-preview and o1-mini consume 2 PRUs per request (double the standard rate). Premium models offer enhanced reasoning for complex tasks.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · GitHub Copilot Across Environments / GitHub Copilot Chat

question: When using GitHub Copilot on GitHub.com, which statement is TRUE about generating a pull request summary?

options.text: PR summary generation consumes Premium Request Units (PRUs), typically 1–2 PRUs depending on complexity
PR summary generation is always free and never consumes PRUs
PR summaries can only be generated from the IDE, not from GitHub.com
PR summaries are available only on the GitHub Copilot Enterprise plan

explanation: The module explicitly notes that PR summary generation is an advanced feature that consumes PRUs — typically 1–2 PRUs depending on the size and complexity of the changes.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · GitHub Copilot Across Environments / GitHub Copilot on GitHub.com

question: In GitHub Copilot CLI, what is the ONLY way to control session settings and configuration such as selecting an AI model or changing the terminal theme?

options.text: Slash commands (e.g., /model, /theme)
Natural language prompts typed at the interactive prompt
Environment variables set before launching Copilot CLI
A GUI settings panel accessible from the terminal

explanation: The module explicitly states that 'Slash commands cannot be replaced with natural language prompts. They are the only way to control session settings and configuration.' Commands like /model and /theme are used for configuration.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · GitHub Copilot Across Environments / GitHub Copilot for the Command Line

question: Which of the following best describes a unique capability of GitHub Copilot CLI cloud sandbox sessions that local sandboxing does NOT provide?

options.text: Sessions run in GitHub-hosted infrastructure, allowing work to be resumed from a different device
Cloud sandboxing restricts Copilot's access to the local filesystem for improved security
Cloud sandboxing is built on local Docker containers managed by the developer
Cloud sandboxing eliminates all PRU consumption for CLI commands

explanation: Cloud sandbox sessions run in GitHub-hosted infrastructure (built on Azure Container Apps Sandboxes), meaning work is not tied to a specific computer — you can start on one device and resume on another. Local sandboxing uses the developer's own machine resources.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · GitHub Copilot Across Environments / GitHub Copilot for the Command Line

question: In the GitHub Copilot app, which session mode has the agent work AUTONOMOUSLY — running tests, iterating on fixes, and completing tasks with minimal developer intervention?

options.text: Autopilot
Interactive
Plan
Supervised

explanation: Autopilot mode lets the agent work autonomously to implement changes, run tests, iterate on fixes, and complete the task with minimal intervention — best for well-defined implementation work. Interactive mode collaborates step-by-step, and Plan mode creates a review-ready plan before executing.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · GitHub Copilot Across Environments / The GitHub Copilot app

question: According to the GitHub Copilot surface comparison, which Copilot experience is BEST suited for orchestrating work end-to-end from issue selection through code, PR, and merge?

options.text: The GitHub Copilot app
Copilot in VS Code (IDE)
Copilot CLI
Copilot on GitHub.com

explanation: The comparison table identifies the GitHub Copilot app as best for 'managing end-to-end agent workflows' with its key role being 'orchestrating work from issue → code → PR → merge.' The IDE is for hands-on editing; CLI is for terminal automation; GitHub.com is for collaboration and planning.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · GitHub Copilot Across Environments / The GitHub Copilot app

question: Which GitHub Copilot plans include the feature 'Data excluded from training by default'?

options.text: Business and Enterprise only
Pro, Business, and Enterprise
Enterprise only
All plans including Free

explanation: According to the management policy features table, 'Data excluded from training by default' is marked available (✅) only for Business and Enterprise, and not available (❌) for Free and Pro plans.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Management and Customization Considerations / Explore GitHub Copilot plans

question: Which GitHub Copilot customization feature is available EXCLUSIVELY on the Enterprise plan?

options.text: Attach knowledge bases to chat for organizational context
Unlimited integrations with Copilot Extensions (public beta)
Build a private extension for internal tooling (public beta)
Public code filter

explanation: The customization features table shows that attaching knowledge bases to chat for organizational context (and tailoring chat to a private codebase) are available only on Enterprise. Copilot Extensions (public and private) are available on all plans.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Management and Customization Considerations / Explore GitHub Copilot plans

question: For GitHub Copilot's IP indemnity to be active on a Business or Enterprise plan, which setting is REQUIRED?

options.text: The 'Matching public code' setting must be set to Block
SAML SSO authentication must be enabled for all users
Data collection and retention must be opted out of by each individual user
The organization must be enrolled in the GitHub Copilot Trust Center program

explanation: The module states that for GitHub to assume legal responsibility under IP indemnity, the 'Matching public code' setting must be set to 'Blocked.' Without this setting, IP indemnity protection does not apply.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Management and Customization Considerations / Contractual protections and disabling matching public code

question: A developer's GitHub Copilot seat is assigned by their organization (not purchased individually). Who controls the 'Suggestions matching public code' toggle for that developer?

options.text: The organization admin controls the setting, and the individual user's toggle may be locked to reflect the organization's policy
The individual user always retains full control over this toggle regardless of how their seat was assigned
GitHub automatically sets it to 'Block' for all org-assigned seats with no override possible
Only GitHub Support can change this setting for org-assigned seats

explanation: For org-provided seats, the toggle may be locked and will reflect the organization's policy. Individual users who purchase their own license (Free, Pro, Pro+) can fully control this setting themselves.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Management and Customization Considerations / Contractual protections and disabling matching public code

question: When a file is excluded via GitHub Copilot's content exclusion feature, which THREE of the following effects occur?

options.text: Code completion is no longer available in the affected files
The content in affected files won't inform code completion suggestions in other files
The content in affected files won't inform GitHub Copilot Chat responses
The file is automatically deleted from the repository

explanation: The module lists exactly three effects of content exclusion: (1) code completion unavailable in excluded files, (2) excluded content doesn't inform suggestions in other files, and (3) excluded content doesn't inform Copilot Chat responses. Files are never deleted.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Management and Customization Considerations / Manage content exclusions

question: In Visual Studio Code, which scenario represents a known LIMITATION of GitHub Copilot content exclusions?

options.text: Content exclusions are not applied when the @github chat participant is used in a question
Content exclusions cannot be configured at the repository level, only at the organization level
Excluded files are still used for Copilot suggestions if they are in the root directory
Content exclusions apply only to Python and JavaScript files

explanation: A documented IDE limitation is that in Visual Studio Code and Visual Studio, content exclusions are not applied when you use the @github chat participant in your question. This means sensitive content may still surface through that specific interaction mode.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Management and Customization Considerations / Manage content exclusions

question: After an organization admin adds or changes a content exclusion, how long can it take for the changes to take effect in IDEs where the settings are already loaded?

options.text: Up to 30 minutes
Up to 5 minutes
Immediately upon saving
Up to 24 hours

explanation: The troubleshooting unit states that content exclusion changes can take up to 30 minutes to take effect in already-loaded IDEs. To apply changes immediately, administrators or users should reload the content exclusion settings in their IDE.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Management and Customization Considerations / Troubleshoot common problems with GitHub Copilot

question: A developer notices that GitHub Copilot is not providing any code suggestions in their editor. Which of the following is listed as a recommended troubleshooting action?

options.text: Review content exclusions to ensure the current file is not excluded from Copilot analysis
Reinstall the operating system to resolve driver conflicts with the Copilot extension
Downgrade to a previous version of the Copilot extension for better compatibility
Switch to a different programming language, as some languages are unsupported

explanation: The troubleshooting unit recommends reviewing content exclusions as one of the primary actions when suggestions are missing — if a file is excluded, suggestions won't appear. Other recommended actions include checking internet connection, updating the extension, and verifying IDE compatibility.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Management and Customization Considerations / Troubleshoot common problems with GitHub Copilot

question: Content exclusion settings configured in an organization's GitHub Copilot settings apply to which users?

options.text: Only members licensed as part of that organization's GitHub Copilot Business or Enterprise subscription
All GitHub users who access any repository in that organization
All GitHub Copilot users globally, regardless of plan or organization
Only organization owners and administrators, not regular developers

explanation: The module specifies that content exclusion settings apply only to members of the organization in which the exclusion is configured — specifically those licensed under a Copilot Business or Enterprise subscription. External users who access the same files can still receive suggestions.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Management and Customization Considerations / Manage content exclusions

question: How does GitHub Copilot Agent Mode differ from the Inline Suggestions feature?

options.text: Agent Mode functions as an autonomous peer programmer that analyzes the entire workspace, executes terminal commands, and iteratively refines its output
Agent Mode provides real-time autocomplete as you type, while Inline Suggestions handle multi-file refactoring
Agent Mode only works on single files, whereas Inline Suggestions can span multiple files
Agent Mode is limited to documentation generation, while Inline Suggestions handle code changes

explanation: The module explicitly states that Inline Suggestions function similarly to traditional autocomplete, while Agent Mode 'takes automation to the next level by orchestrating development tasks dynamically' and 'not only refines its own outputs but also iterates multiple times to improve accuracy.'
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Building Applications with GitHub Copilot Agent Mode / What is GitHub Copilot Agent Mode?

question: Which sequence correctly describes how Agent Mode processes a task?

options.text: Determines relevant files and dependencies → suggests and executes code changes → runs terminal commands → monitors and refines output
Runs terminal commands → determines relevant files → suggests code changes → submits a pull request for review
Generates documentation → refactors code → determines dependencies → runs tests
Asks the developer to select files → writes code → monitors output → closes the task

explanation: The unit lists the four steps in exact order: determine files and dependencies, suggest and execute changes aligned with project structure, run terminal commands (compile, install, test), then monitor and refine output iteratively.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Building Applications with GitHub Copilot Agent Mode / What is GitHub Copilot Agent Mode?

question: A developer uses Agent Mode's draft-review workflow. Approximately how many Premium Request Units (PRUs) does a standard two-step draft–review sequence consume?

options.text: 2–3 PRUs
Exactly 1 PRU
4–6 PRUs
No PRUs; only GitHub Actions minutes are consumed

explanation: The module states that 'each handoff in Agent Mode consumes ~1 PRU' and that 'a 2-step draft–review sequence typically uses 2–3 PRUs.'
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Building Applications with GitHub Copilot Agent Mode / Explore the power of autonomous development assistance

question: When Agent Mode generates unit tests that initially fail due to a syntax error, what does it do next?

options.text: It autonomously detects the cause of failure, applies a corrective solution, and re-runs the tests until they pass
It stops execution and waits for the developer to manually fix the error before continuing
It discards the failing tests and regenerates new ones from scratch
It raises a terminal warning and reverts all changes to the previous state

explanation: The 'self-healing' section of the unit describes Agent Mode's iterative process: it detects the failure, applies a fix, and re-runs the tests until they pass, minimising manual debugging effort.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Building Applications with GitHub Copilot Agent Mode / Explore the power of autonomous development assistance

question: How does Agent Mode achieve context-aware deployment for a React application?

options.text: It recognises the project type via package.json and then runs the suitable build scripts aligned with the existing workflow context
It asks the developer to specify the framework before running any commands
It searches for a README.md file to identify the project type and deployment target
It uses the IDE's active language extension to determine the correct build pipeline

explanation: The unit states that Agent Mode 'recognises project type via package.json' and then 'runs suitable build scripts' aligned with existing workflow contexts to provide accurate outputs.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Building Applications with GitHub Copilot Agent Mode / Explore the power of autonomous development assistance

question: Which scenario best illustrates a known limitation of GitHub Copilot Agent Mode?

options.text: The developer provides a task involving poorly documented custom business logic, resulting in less accurate solutions and increased need for manual review
Agent Mode refuses to run terminal commands unless the developer explicitly approves each command
Agent Mode cannot install npm dependencies and requires the developer to run npm install manually
Agent Mode can only modify a maximum of three files per task

explanation: The module notes that Agent Mode 'may struggle with specialized domain logic, nuanced business rules, or when critical project context is missing,' and gives poorly documented custom business logic as the example limitation.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Building Applications with GitHub Copilot Agent Mode / Explore the power of autonomous development assistance

question: What premium-reasoning cost should developers expect when using an advanced model in Agent Mode compared to the standard model?

options.text: Premium reasoning often doubles PRU consumption, with a single request using approximately 4+ PRUs versus approximately 2 PRUs with the standard model
Premium reasoning costs the same as the standard model but increases response latency
Premium reasoning consumes GitHub Actions minutes instead of PRUs
Premium reasoning is free for Copilot Enterprise users and only costs PRUs on lower-tier plans

explanation: The module states that 'premium reasoning (using more advanced models)… often doubles PRU consumption. A single request may use ~4+ PRUs compared to ~2 with the standard model.'
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Building Applications with GitHub Copilot Agent Mode / Explore the power of autonomous development assistance

question: What is the key architectural distinction between the GitHub Copilot Cloud Agent and the agent mode feature in Visual Studio Code?

options.text: The Cloud Agent runs autonomously in a GitHub Actions-powered environment to complete tasks assigned through issues or Copilot Chat, while agent mode performs autonomous local edits directly in the IDE session
The Cloud Agent only supports documentation updates, while agent mode handles code changes
Agent mode requires a GitHub Actions runner, while the Cloud Agent runs inside the local IDE
The Cloud Agent is available only on Copilot Enterprise, while agent mode works on all plans

explanation: The module explicitly distinguishes the two: 'Cloud agent: Runs autonomously in a GitHub Actions-powered environment… Agent mode (Copilot Edits): Performs autonomous local edits directly in your IDE session.'
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Accelerate Development with GitHub Copilot Cloud Agent / Understanding and Enabling the GitHub Copilot Cloud Agent

question: To which branch names is the Copilot Cloud Agent restricted when pushing code changes?

options.text: Branches whose names begin with copilot/
Any feature branch, as long as it is not the default branch
Branches prefixed with ai/ or bot/
Any branch selected by the developer when assigning the issue

explanation: The security model states that 'pushes are restricted to copilot/ branches (not main/master),' ensuring the agent cannot modify protected default branches.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Accelerate Development with GitHub Copilot Cloud Agent / Security, Risks, and Limitations

question: What is the first signal that Copilot Cloud Agent has received and acknowledged an assigned issue?

options.text: Copilot adds an 👀 reaction to the issue
Copilot immediately opens a merged pull request
Copilot sends an email notification to the assignor
A 'Copilot finished work' event appears in the repository timeline

explanation: The tracking section states: 'Shortly after you assign an issue, Copilot adds an 👀 reaction to the issue' as the immediate confirmation of acknowledgement.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Accelerate Development with GitHub Copilot Cloud Agent / Assigning, Tracking, and Troubleshooting

question: Which two cost resources does Copilot Cloud Agent consume when performing a task?

options.text: GitHub Actions minutes
Copilot Premium Request Units (PRUs)
Azure Compute credits
GitHub storage bandwidth

explanation: The module states that 'Copilot Cloud Agent uses two main resources: GitHub Actions minutes for the ephemeral build/test environment… and Copilot Premium Requests (PRUs) to power advanced model reasoning.'
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Accelerate Development with GitHub Copilot Cloud Agent / Understanding and Enabling the GitHub Copilot Cloud Agent

question: Why can the developer who requested a Copilot Cloud Agent pull request NOT approve it?

options.text: To preserve the repository's required-reviews rules and ensure an independent review before merge
Because the developer lacks write permission once the agent takes over the branch
Because the pull request is always merged automatically after the agent finishes
GitHub prevents the same user from both opening and approving any pull request

explanation: The module states that 'the developer who asked Copilot to create the pull request cannot approve it, which preserves your repository's required reviews rules and ensures an independent review before merge.'
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Accelerate Development with GitHub Copilot Cloud Agent / Assigning, Tracking, and Troubleshooting

question: What file must you create—and what job name must it contain—to pre-install tools and dependencies before Copilot Cloud Agent starts a session?

options.text: .github/workflows/copilot-setup-steps.yml containing a job named copilot-setup-steps
.github/copilot-instructions.md containing a YAML setup block
.github/workflows/agent-bootstrap.yml containing a job named agent-init
copilot.config.json in the repository root listing pre-install commands

explanation: The customization unit specifies: 'Create .github/workflows/copilot-setup-steps.yml on your repository's default branch. The workflow must define a single job named copilot-setup-steps.'
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Accelerate Development with GitHub Copilot Cloud Agent / Customizing, Extending, and Validating

question: Which types of MCP features does Copilot Cloud Agent support?

options.text: MCP tools only; MCP resources and prompts are not supported, and remote MCP servers requiring OAuth are not supported
MCP tools, resources, and prompts, with full OAuth support for remote servers
MCP prompts only; tools must be invoked manually via the CLI
MCP resources only, giving the agent read-only access to external data stores

explanation: The unit explicitly notes: 'Copilot Cloud Agent supports MCP tools only (not resources or prompts). Remote MCP servers that require OAuth aren't supported.'
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Accelerate Development with GitHub Copilot Cloud Agent / Customizing, Extending, and Validating

question: A team notices their Copilot Cloud Agent pull requests consistently fail CI because the agent does not follow the project's linting rules. What is the recommended corrective action?

options.text: Iterate instructions in .github/copilot-instructions.md so the agent can self-validate with tests and linters
Disable all branch protection rules so the agent can merge without CI checks
Switch to a self-hosted runner that has the linter pre-installed
Change the AI model used by the agent in organization settings to a more capable version

explanation: The validation best-practices section advises: 'Iterate instructions in .github/copilot-instructions.md when you see repeated mistakes,' and the troubleshooting section says to 'Provide clear repo-level guidance via .github/copilot-instructions.md so the agent can self-validate with tests/linters.'
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Accelerate Development with GitHub Copilot Cloud Agent / Customizing, Extending, and Validating

question: How does the MCP module describe the Model Context Protocol in terms of an everyday analogy?

options.text: As a USB-C standard for AI tools, providing a consistent and secure way for AI models to connect to the tools and data sources they need
As an HTTP API gateway that routes requests between AI models and enterprise databases
As a virtual machine layer that isolates AI model execution from the host operating system
As a publish-subscribe message bus connecting cloud-based AI services to on-premises data

explanation: The module states directly: 'MCP (Model Context Protocol) is like a USB-C standard for your AI tools, providing a consistent and secure way for AI models to connect to the tools and data sources they need.'

question: What is the correct Server URL to enter when setting up GitHub MCP Server in VS Code using the OAuth method?

https://github.com/mcp/server
https://copilot.github.com/api/mcp
https://mcp.github.dev/connect

question: Which authentication method is required when running GitHub MCP Server locally using Docker?

options.text: A Personal Access Token (PAT); OAuth is not supported in the local Docker setup
OAuth via a one-click browser login, the same as the hosted setup
A GitHub App installation token generated from organization settings
No authentication is required for a local Docker installation

question: What PAT scopes are needed when configuring GitHub MCP Server in VS Code with a Personal Access Token?

options.text: repo and read:packages
repo and write:packages
admin:org and repo
read:user and read:org

explanation: The unit instructs: 'Create a PAT with repo and read:packages scope in your GitHub account.'
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Introduction to MCP Server / Configure, Connect, and Use GitHub MCP Server in VS Code

question: Within VS Code, what step must you take in Copilot Chat before MCP Server tools become available for use?

options.text: Switch Copilot Chat to Agent mode
Open the Command Palette and run 'MCP: Enable Tools'
Restart VS Code after the MCP Server is configured
Sign out and back into your GitHub account within VS Code

explanation: The unit's usage instructions state: 'Open Copilot Chat in Visual Studio Code and switch to Agent mode to activate the MCP Server tools.'

question: What does the concept of an 'agentic loop' mean when MCP is combined with Copilot's agent mode?

options.text: Copilot dynamically seeks information, analyzes results, and makes informed next steps—all without restarting the process from scratch
Copilot repeatedly re-prompts the user until a satisfactory answer is confirmed
A scheduled GitHub Actions workflow that periodically runs Copilot to check for new issues
A feedback loop in which Copilot Chat responses are automatically submitted as pull request comments

explanation: The unit describes the agentic loop as: 'Copilot isn't just reacting to a single prompt. Instead, it's working in a cycle: exploring, adapting, and refining until it produces the outcome you want.' It 'dynamically seeks information, analyzes results, and makes informed next steps, all without restarting the process from scratch.'
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Introduction to MCP Server / Using GitHub MCP Server with Copilot Chat

question: Which of the following is an advantage of using the GitHub-hosted MCP Server over running an MCP server locally?

options.text: The hosted server requires no Docker installation or manual configuration files and provides one-click OAuth login
The hosted server stores your code locally to prevent data from leaving the machine
The hosted server supports PAT authentication only, making it more secure than OAuth
The hosted server is only available to GitHub Enterprise Server customers

explanation: The module states that local MCP servers 'typically requires Docker, token management, and manual configuration,' whereas 'connecting to the GitHub-hosted server is quick and easy with no need for docker or config files' and provides 'easy one-click OAuth login.'

question: When GitHub Copilot is assigned as a reviewer to a pull request on GitHub.com, what type of review outcome does it produce?

options.text: A comment review only — it never approves or rejects the pull request
An approval that counts toward the repository's required approvals
A rejection that blocks the merge until the issues are resolved
A draft approval that becomes final after a human co-signs it

explanation: Copilot generates a comment review—not an approval or rejection—so it never blocks merges but instead adds valuable context for human reviewers. Its comments do not count toward required approvals.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Leveling Up Code Reviews and Pull Requests with GitHub Copilot / Using Copilot as a reviewer in GitHub.com

question: How does a developer request a GitHub Copilot code review on GitHub.com?

options.text: Add Copilot from the Reviewers menu in the pull request, just like assigning a human teammate
Mention @github-copilot in any pull request comment
Enable the option under repository Settings → Actions → AI Reviews
Run the 'gh copilot review' CLI command against the feature branch

explanation: On GitHub.com, requesting a Copilot review is as simple as selecting Copilot from the Reviewers menu. Copilot typically finishes the review in less than 30 seconds.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Leveling Up Code Reviews and Pull Requests with GitHub Copilot / Using Copilot as a reviewer in GitHub.com

question: Which file should a development team create to provide GitHub Copilot with repository-specific review guidelines?

options.text: .github/copilot-instructions.md
.copilot/review-rules.json
.github/CODEOWNERS
.vscode/copilot.settings.json

explanation: Teams create a .github/copilot-instructions.md file to customize Copilot's review behavior, directing it to look for the same patterns the team does and keeping suggestions aligned with their standards for readability, security, or style.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Leveling Up Code Reviews and Pull Requests with GitHub Copilot / Using Copilot as a reviewer in GitHub.com

question: When configuring path-specific custom instructions for GitHub Copilot code review, which frontmatter keyword specifies the target files or directories?

options.text: applyTo
targetPath
scope
include

explanation: Path-specific instruction files are placed in .github/instructions/ with names ending in .instructions.md. Each file must include a frontmatter block with the 'applyTo' keyword using glob syntax (e.g., 'app/models/**/*.rb') to specify which paths the instructions apply to.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Leveling Up Code Reviews and Pull Requests with GitHub Copilot / Catching issues early and automating reviews with Copilot

question: A developer wants to enable automatic Copilot reviews on every pull request they personally open across all their repositories. Which GitHub Copilot plan is required for this personal account feature?

options.text: Copilot Pro or Copilot Pro+
Any plan, including Copilot Free
Copilot Business or Copilot Enterprise only
Copilot Pro+, Business, and Enterprise only

explanation: The automatic Copilot code review option for a personal account is only available on the Copilot Pro or Copilot Pro+ plans. It is enabled through the user's personal Copilot settings page.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Leveling Up Code Reviews and Pull Requests with GitHub Copilot / Catching issues early and automating reviews with Copilot

question: A repository administrator wants every pull request targeting the default branch to automatically receive a Copilot review. What is the correct mechanism to enforce this?

options.text: Create a branch ruleset that requires a pull request and enables 'Request pull request review from Copilot'
Add a GitHub Actions workflow that calls the Copilot API on pull_request events
Add Copilot to the repository's CODEOWNERS file for all paths using the pattern '*'
Enable automatic reviews under repository Settings → Code Security → AI Features

explanation: Repository admins enforce automatic Copilot reviews by creating a branch ruleset, enabling 'Require a pull request before merging', then selecting 'Request pull request review from Copilot' in the expanded options. This ensures every PR targeting the specified branch is reviewed.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Leveling Up Code Reviews and Pull Requests with GitHub Copilot / Catching issues early and automating reviews with Copilot

question: What is the key difference between what GitHub Copilot can do with Premium Request Units (PRUs) versus without them?

options.text: With PRUs, Copilot can scan entire diffs, apply custom guidelines, and return context-rich fixes; without them, it provides only default lightweight suggestions
PRUs allow Copilot to approve or reject pull requests; without PRUs it can only leave comments
PRUs enable Copilot to work inside IDEs; without them it is limited to GitHub.com only
PRUs give Copilot access to private repositories; standard requests are limited to public repos

explanation: PRUs unlock Copilot's advanced reasoning: with PRUs it can analyze large diffs, interpret custom review guidelines, and produce context-rich actionable fixes. Without them, Copilot provides only its default lightweight suggestions.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Leveling Up Code Reviews and Pull Requests with GitHub Copilot / Measuring impact and optimizing premium request units (PRUs)

question: According to the module, what is the recommended strategy for optimizing Premium Request Unit (PRU) consumption?

options.text: Reserve PRU-powered reviews for large or high-risk changes, and rely on standard Copilot suggestions for simple edits
Disable .github/copilot-instructions.md so that every review runs on the lightweight model
Limit Copilot reviews to the main branch only so fewer PRUs are spent overall
Use Copilot Chat for all interactions to avoid triggering IDE-based PRU consumption

explanation: The module recommends using PRUs strategically: reserving premium reviews for large or high-risk changes while using standard suggestions for simple edits, and setting usage alerts at 75%, 90%, and 100% of monthly PRU consumption to track spending.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Leveling Up Code Reviews and Pull Requests with GitHub Copilot / Measuring impact and optimizing premium request units (PRUs)

question: In Visual Studio Code, how do you accept a GitHub Copilot code suggestion?

options.text: Press the Tab key
Press Enter
Press Ctrl+Space to commit the highlighted suggestion
Double-click the grey suggestion text

explanation: Copilot suggestions appear as grey text in the editor. To accept the suggestion, press the Tab key.

question: When GitHub Copilot offers multiple JavaScript code suggestions in VS Code, which keyboard shortcut lets you cycle through them?

options.text: Ctrl+Enter (Cmd+Enter on Mac)
Alt+] to go forward, Alt+[ to go back
Ctrl+Shift+Space to open a suggestion picker panel
F2 to cycle through inline completions

explanation: To cycle through multiple Copilot suggestions, use Ctrl+Enter (or Cmd+Enter on Mac). This lets you view various suggestions and select the most appropriate one.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Using GitHub Copilot with JavaScript / What is GitHub Copilot

question: A developer writes the JavaScript comment '// Create an API endpoint' and finds Copilot's response unhelpful. What is the most likely reason?

options.text: The prompt is too vague; Copilot needs specific details like the framework and expected request type
JavaScript comments are not recognized as prompts; the developer must use Copilot Chat instead
Copilot does not support API generation; it is limited to utility functions
The prompt must be placed inside a function body, not at the top level of the file

explanation: Ambiguous prompts like '// Create an API endpoint' give Copilot insufficient context, leading to suggestions that may use an unknown framework or unexpected data. A specific prompt—such as one naming the framework and request type—allows Copilot to understand the goal and scope of the task.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Using GitHub Copilot with JavaScript / Use GitHub Copilot with JavaScript

question: How does GitHub Copilot use files that are currently open in your VS Code editor (beyond the file you are actively editing)?

options.text: As additional context that informs and improves Copilot's suggestions
As files Copilot will automatically refactor when a suggestion is accepted
Only as language-detection hints; their code content is not analyzed
As test fixtures that Copilot runs in the background to validate its output

explanation: GitHub Copilot uses all open files in the editor as additional context, providing useful information beyond the immediate prompt or code being written. Opening related files therefore helps Copilot generate more relevant suggestions.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Using GitHub Copilot with JavaScript / Use GitHub Copilot with JavaScript

question: In which of the following ways can GitHub Copilot recognize that text is a prompt in a Python project? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Typed as a comment in a .py code file
Typed as text in a Markdown file, pausing a few seconds for Copilot to respond
Spoken using VS Code's voice-input accessibility feature
Typed as a variable name prefixed with a double underscore

explanation: Copilot recognizes prompts when they appear as comments in code files (.py, .js, etc.) or as text typed in a Markdown file after which you pause briefly so Copilot can generate a response.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Using GitHub Copilot with Python / What is GitHub Copilot?

question: A Python developer writes the comment '# Create an API endpoint using the FastAPI framework that accepts a JSON payload in a POST request'. Why is this a better Copilot prompt than '# Create an API endpoint'?

options.text: It is specific and clear, giving Copilot the framework and request details needed to match the developer's intent
It is longer, and Copilot always produces better output for longer comments
It uses the word 'endpoint', which Copilot's model recognizes as a special keyword
It avoids generic terms, which trigger Copilot's safe-mode response filter

explanation: A vague prompt may cause Copilot to use an unrecognized framework or unfamiliar data shapes. A specific prompt that names the framework (FastAPI) and request type (POST with JSON payload) lets Copilot understand both the goal and the scope, producing a higher-quality output.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Using GitHub Copilot with Python / Use GitHub Copilot with Python

question: According to GitHub Copilot best practices for Python, what is the recommended approach when building a complex prompt?

options.text: Start with a simple, high-level prompt and then iteratively add more specific details
Write the full detailed specification in a single lengthy comment before generating any code
Let Copilot generate a complete first draft, then start a fresh session for corrections
Reserve inline comments for simple completions and use only Copilot Chat for complex tasks

explanation: The module recommends starting with a simple prompt and then elaborating with additional requirements in follow-up prompts. This iterative approach guides Copilot toward progressively more specific and useful suggestions.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Using GitHub Copilot with Python / Use GitHub Copilot with Python

question: When GitHub Copilot's Python suggestions are not what a developer needs, what does the module recommend?

options.text: Reword the prompt, or start writing code manually for Copilot to autocomplete
Restart VS Code to reset Copilot's context window
Close all other open files so Copilot has less conflicting context
Switch to a different programming language file and re-import the logic

explanation: If suggestions are unhelpful, the module advises rewording the prompt to be clearer and more specific, or beginning to write the code yourself so Copilot can autocomplete from the partial implementation.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Using GitHub Copilot with Python / Use GitHub Copilot with Python

question: For members of an organization with a Copilot Business or Copilot Enterprise plan, what prerequisite must be in place before MCP can be used with GitHub Copilot?

options.text: The 'MCP servers in Copilot' policy must be enabled by organization or enterprise administrators
Each developer must individually opt in by visiting their personal Copilot settings page
The repository must have a .github/mcp-config.json file committed to the default branch
A GitHub App with MCP permissions must be installed on the organization

explanation: If you're a member of an organization or enterprise with a Copilot Business or Copilot Enterprise plan, the 'MCP servers in Copilot' policy must be enabled in order to use MCP with Copilot.

question: Which capability of GitHub Copilot most directly addresses the productivity drain caused by context switching?

options.text: Providing code suggestions directly in the IDE, minimizing the need to search for solutions online
Automatically deploying code to staging environments after each save
Running static-analysis scans as a background IDE process
Synchronizing open editor tabs across multiple devices

explanation: GitHub Copilot's in-editor assistance keeps developers in their workflow by surfacing suggestions and API references without requiring them to leave the IDE to consult external documentation.

question: A team is bootstrapping a new microservice. According to the module, what can GitHub Copilot generate from a few descriptive comments about the service requirements?

Live production traffic data and analytics dashboards
Real authentication tokens and signed SSL certificates
Domain registration and DNS zone files

question: How does the module characterize GitHub Copilot's role in the debugging process?

options.text: It is not a full debugger, but it can provide plain-language error explanations and suggest log statements to help diagnose issues
It functions as a fully integrated debugger that sets breakpoints and steps through code automatically
It connects to a remote debug server to analyze runtime state
It rewrites any function that contains a bug without developer input

question: Which stage of the Software Development Lifecycle (SDLC) does GitHub Copilot directly enhance by generating test cases based on function signatures, creating realistic test data sets, and suggesting edge-case test scenarios?

options.text: Testing & quality assurance
Requirement analysis
Deployment
Maintenance & support

question: Which statement accurately describes a limitation of GitHub Copilot related to its training data?

options.text: Suggestions may reflect biases or outdated practices in the training data, and there are ongoing copyright concerns about code generated from trained models
GitHub Copilot cannot suggest code in more than three programming languages simultaneously
GitHub Copilot requires an internet connection only during its initial model-training phase
GitHub Copilot's suggestions are always synchronized with the latest framework release notes

question: In the four-stage GitHub Copilot measurement framework, what is the focus of the Evaluation stage?

options.text: Leading indicators such as developer satisfaction and task completion rates, plus API metrics like Average Daily Active Users, Total Acceptance Rate, and Lines of Code Accepted
Deploying GitHub Copilot immediately to all enterprise developers to maximize coverage
Disabling Copilot for a control group to establish a productivity baseline
Fine-tuning Copilot's impact on broader goals such as reducing time-to-market

explanation: The Evaluation stage is the initial adoption phase; it focuses on leading indicators (developer satisfaction, task completion) and uses the REST API to collect metrics such as Average Daily Active Users, Total Acceptance Rate, and Lines of Code Accepted.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Developer use cases for AI with GitHub Copilot / Understand limitations and measure impact

question: A survey program manager wants to gather detailed insights about GitHub Copilot's impact on team dynamics without causing survey fatigue. What is the maximum recommended frequency for deploying the long-form GitHub Copilot Developer Survey?

options.text: No more than once every four weeks
Every two weeks
Once per quarter
Daily, using automated survey tooling

question: Which set of unit test frameworks is supported by the C# Dev Kit extension in Visual Studio Code?

options.text: xUnit, NUnit, and MSTest
Jasmine, Mocha, and Jest
PyTest, Unittest, and Nose
JUnit, TestNG, and Mockito

question: What is the primary function of the Test Explorer view provided by the C# Dev Kit in Visual Studio Code?

options.text: A tree view that shows all test cases in the workspace, allowing you to run or debug individual tests and view pass/fail results
A specialized code editor optimized for writing assertion statements
A dedicated terminal emulator for running dotnet test commands
A code-coverage heatmap that highlights lines not yet covered by tests

explanation: Test Explorer is described as a tree view that shows all test cases in your workspace, with options to run or debug tests and view results including pass/fail icons and durations.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Develop unit tests using GitHub Copilot tools / Examine Visual Studio Code support for unit testing

question: When generating unit tests from the GitHub Copilot Chat view, which agent and permission level does the module recommend as the starting point?

options.text: Agent mode with Default Approvals
Ask mode with Bypass Approvals
Plan mode with Autopilot
Agent mode with Bypass Approvals

explanation: The module states that Agent mode with Default Approvals is the recommended starting point because the Agent can edit files, run terminal commands, and rerun tests, while Default Approvals keeps the developer in the loop by confirming each tool invocation.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Develop unit tests using GitHub Copilot tools / Generate unit tests with the GitHub Copilot Chat view

question: A developer is onboarding a legacy project that has no unit tests. Which GitHub Copilot Chat slash command recommends a test framework and walks through configuration, including installing packages and scaffolding the test project?

options.text: /setupTests
/tests
/fixTestFailure
/plan

explanation: The /setupTests slash command recommends a framework and, in Agent mode, installs packages, scaffolds the test project, and recommends VS Code testing extensions. It is most useful when starting a new test project or onboarding a project that doesn't yet include tests.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Develop unit tests using GitHub Copilot tools / Generate unit tests with the GitHub Copilot Chat view

question: How does the Plan agent in the GitHub Copilot Chat view differ from the Agent when applied to unit testing tasks?

options.text: Plan produces a detailed step-by-step implementation plan for review before any code is written; Agent autonomously implements, runs, and iterates on tests across multiple files
Plan writes tests directly into the project files; Agent only answers read-only questions
Plan is limited to JavaScript projects; Agent supports all languages
Plan uses fewer premium requests than Agent for the same testing task

explanation: Plan is for structured, step-by-step implementation plans you review before implementation, while Agent handles autonomous, multi-file coding workflows including running tests and iterating on failures.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Develop unit tests using GitHub Copilot tools / Plan and automate test workflows using the Plan and Agent modes

question: A developer has a test file with three passing tests and wants to quickly add edge-case tests without opening the Chat view. What is the fastest method the module recommends?

options.text: Use ghost text (inline code completion) by positioning the cursor after the last test and typing a descriptive comment to trigger a suggestion
Run the /tests slash command in the Chat view for the entire file
Use the Fix Test Failure button in Test Explorer to generate additional cases
Use the Plan agent to redesign the full test strategy first

explanation: Ghost text is called the fastest way to extend coverage once initial tests are in place. It uses existing test patterns in the file to suggest similar test cases as the developer types, without leaving the editor.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Develop unit tests using GitHub Copilot tools / Extend tests with ghost text and fix failing tests

question: When a test fails and appears in Test Explorer, what one-click GitHub Copilot feature opens a chat session with the failing test and its error output already attached as context?

options.text: The Fix Test Failure button (sparkle icon) that appears when hovering over the failing test in Test Explorer
The Debug Test option available by right-clicking the play button next to the test method
The Test: Show Output command in the Command Palette
The Coverage Gutters extension's failing-line indicator

explanation: Hovering over a failing test in Test Explorer and selecting the Fix Test Failure button (sparkle icon) triggers GitHub Copilot to open a chat session with the failing test and its output already attached.
source: Copilot Fundamentals · Develop unit tests using GitHub Copilot tools / Extend tests with ghost text and fix failing tests

question: What are the benefits of using implicit prompts with slash commands in inline chat for fixing code issues with GitHub Copilot?

options.text: Implicit prompts help get better responses from GitHub Copilot without writing longer prompts, making it easier to interact and fix code issues.
Implicit prompts help enforce a consistent naming convention and syntax based on best practices within Visual Studio Code projects for improved readability.
Implicit prompts help detect security vulnerabilities and potential malicious activities within Visual Studio Code projects for increased safety.

explanation: Slash commands act as implicit prompts, getting better responses without long typed prompts.

question: What is LoRA in the context of fine-tuning Large Language Models (LLMs)?

options.text: A method that adds trainable elements to each layer of the pretrained model without a complete overhaul.
A technology optimizing communication between different coding languages.
A specialized software library enhancing Copilot's performance.
A new programming paradigm supported exclusively by Copilot.

explanation: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) adds small trainable elements per layer instead of full retraining.

question: How does Copilot use the context to provide code suggestions?

options.text: It considers the surrounding code, file type, and content of parallel open tabs in the code editor.
It considers only the prompt text you provide.
It considers the file type but not the content of the file.
It randomly selects context from the internet.

question: Which of these strategies helps to improve prompt effectiveness in GitHub Copilot?

Making the prompt as general as possible.
Keeping the prompt lengthy and detailed.
Avoiding examples in the prompt to not restrict Copilot's creativity.

question: Which statement about Copilot Space ownership and description is true?

options.text: You can choose personal or organization ownership (where available), and the description is for human readers
A Space must be organization-owned; personal Spaces aren't supported
The description changes Copilot's answers in the Space
Ownership can't be changed once set, and descriptions affect answer quality

question: Which action does NOT add usable context for Copilot in a Space?

Attaching files or folders from a GitHub repository
Pasting URLs of GitHub issues and pull requests

question: Which prompting pattern best supports runnable, verifiable outputs in a Copilot Space?

Ask for a summary without constraints to keep the model creative
Use many broad instructions to widen the context as much as possible
Avoid referencing attached sources to prevent overfitting

explanation: Concrete constraints plus requests for executable, referenced output yield verifiable results.

Add more examples to increase context so the model has more to learn from

Turn off repository linking so the Space doesn't change
